title: is vs == : Identity vs Equality

overview.what: The `==` operator compares the VALUES of two objects (equality), while the `is` operator compares the MEMORY ADDRESSES of two objects (identity). Two distinct objects can have the exact same value (`==` is True), but because they live in different places in memory, they are not the same object (`is` is False). Think of `==` as asking 'Do these two houses look identical?' and `is` as asking 'Are these two addresses pointing to the exact same house?'. In Python, `is` checks if `id(a) == id(b)`.
overview.why: Mixing up `is` and `==` causes subtle bugs. Use `==` when you want to know if the data matches. Only use `is` when you need to know if two variables refer to the exact same object in memory (most commonly, checking `is None`).

why_learning_this: Preventing subtle bugs when comparing collections

common_mistakes.title: Using `is` for value comparison
common_mistakes.explanation: Using `is` instead of `==` for strings or numbers might sometimes work due to Python caching (interning) small integers and short strings, but it will randomly fail for larger values or dynamic inputs. Always use `==` for value comparison.

common_mistakes.title: Using `==` to check for None
common_mistakes.explanation: While `x == None` works, the Pythonic and safer way is `x is None`. Some objects (like pandas DataFrames or SQLAlchemy models) override `==` to return an array of booleans or a query expression, which breaks the truth value testing. `is None` cannot be overridden.

recall_questions.q: What is the difference between `==` and `is`?
recall_questions.answer: `==` checks if the values are equal (equality), while `is` checks if they are the exact same object in memory (identity).

recall_questions.q: When is it appropriate to use the `is` operator?
recall_questions.answer: When you need to check if two variables point to the exact same object, most commonly when checking for singletons like `None`.

understanding_checks.question: What will the following code output?
understanding_checks.code:
a = [1, 2]
b = [1, 2]
print(a == b)
print(a is b)
understanding_checks.why: `a` and `b` have the same values, so `a == b` is `True`. However, they are two distinct list objects in memory, so `a is b` evaluates to `False`.

understanding_checks.question: Why is it recommended to use `x is None` instead of `x == None`?
understanding_checks.answer: Because `is` guarantees an identity check, preventing bugs if an object overrides `==` to behave unexpectedly.
understanding_checks.why: Using `== None` triggers the `__eq__` method, which some libraries override to return custom objects instead of a simple boolean. `is None` directly compares memory addresses and cannot be overridden.

practice_tasks.title: Fix the singleton comparison
practice_tasks.prompt: The `process_data` function currently uses `==` to check if the result is `None`. This works most of the time but can be unsafe. Modify the function to use the correct operator for checking identity against singletons like `None`.

code_walkthrough.code:
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = [1, 2, 3]
print(a == b)
print(a is b)

aha_moment.code:
a = 256
b = 256
print(a is b)

x = 257
y = 257
print(x is y)
aha_moment.prediction: What will these two prints output?
aha_moment.why: Python pre-caches (interns) small integers (-5 to 256) for performance. So `256` reuses the same memory object (True), but `257` creates a new object in memory each time, so `is` returns False. This is why you must never use `is` for value comparison.

challenge.title: Safe None Checking
challenge.prompt: You are writing a database connector. Write a function `process_record(record)` that takes a record (which could be a dictionary or `None`). If `record` is exactly `None`, return the string 'No data'. Otherwise, return the string 'Processing'. You must use the safest comparison operator for `None`.
